data.title: Anbio Biotechnology's Hype And Hard Approvals Suggest A Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Anbio Biotechnology (NASDAQ:NNNN) is a medical instruments company that develops in-vitro (IVD) test devices. Its solutions can be useful for over-the-counter (OTC), point-of-care (POC), and lab applications. Additionally, NNNN's EU sales depend on upgrading legacy IVDD marks to IVDR, which requires securing a Notified Body and clearing a higher clinical-evidence bar. As for the US, NNNN's commercialization efforts still await approval and are payor-dependent. It's also worth mentioning that NNNN's governance remains a risk, as approximately 99% of voting power is in the Class B shares. Plus, it's incredibly challenging to justify their current valuation even under optimistic growth assumptions. So, I have to remain bearish on the stock at these levels.

IVD Medical Devices

Anbio Biotechnology is a medical device firm focused on in vitro diagnostics (IVD). NNNN produces tests and instruments for over-the-counter (OTC) and laboratory use. The company was incorporated in 2021 as a private Cayman Islands firm, and it had its IPO on the Nasdaq in February 2025. NNNN has executive offices in Frankfurt am Main, Germany. I previously covered NNNN last June, and since then, the stock has massively appreciated by around 254%. However, a lot of the concerns I had back then still remain, so I thought it would be worthwhile to write an update.

As a quick recap, NNNN's portfolio presents a broad set of biomarkers for detecting a wide range of conditions. This includes infectious diseases, oncology conditions, cardiovascular and endocrine disorders, inflammation, renal disease, diabetes, pharmacogenomics (genetics that affect a person's response to medicines), and drug-of-abuse testing. NNNN's strategy centers on affordable and portable testing platforms to allow diagnostics closer to patients for OTC and point-of-care testing (POCT). However, going forward, its growth will depend on successfully scaling up its non-COVID assays (more on this later).

Furthermore, NNNN's product lines include a fluorescent immunoassay (FIA) platform that uses antibodies plus a fluorescent label to detect and quantify targets. Such targets could include antigens or a particular antibody in a patient sample. NNNN's FIA then uses a small reader and analyzer to measure fluorescence to obtain an accurate numeric result. Interestingly, this platform can be applied to detect infectious diseases like influenza A/B, COVID-19, RSV, and dengue, among others. Also, it could be helpful for evaluating cardiac, endocrine, fertility, and inflammatory conditions.

Another product line comprises a dry chemistry platform, with a portable analyzer and a menu for chemistry panels to be used at POCT and for laboratory use. This technology uses dry reagents like slides, strips, and discs instead of liquid reagents. The patient sample is applied to a disposable element, where the reaction occurs, and the device reads the results. This platform is used to evaluate metabolic and organ function with measurement of glucose, urea, creatinine, uric acid, liver panel, lipids, electrolytes, and other options. The dry chemistry platform is used in POC settings, small & medium labs, physician offices, veterinary labs, and in general in resource-limited sites that benefit from room-temperature reagents.

Additionally, NNNN's chemiluminescent immunoassay (ChLIA) solution is a platform that uses antibodies tagged with an enzyme or label that produces light during a chemical reaction. The analyzer measures that light and converts the intensity into a quantitative result for the target to classify between an antigen and an antibody. This solution is used to test endocrine function, hormones, cardiac markers, infectious disease serology, oncology and tumor markers, and inflammation (CRP, PCT). ChLIA offers higher analytical sensitivity and tighter precision than FIA, but FIA wins on portability. ChLIA is ideal for core and midsize labs and near-patient POCT, so at the end of the day, its competitiveness depends on the final use case.

Concentrated Control On Class B Shares

Since June, NNNN's share price has been incredibly volatile. In my view, the stock price is prone to such outsized moves because only a small slice of the total outstanding shares actually trade. Remember, there are only 1.6 million shares from the IPO, but the 20-F disclosed a weighted average shares outstanding of 42.3 million. Thus, when supply is thin, even small buy/sell orders can quickly push the stock price.

Also, NNNN has a dual-class setup that places around 99% of voting power with two Class B holders. These are CVC Investment and Northwestern Investment, and their Class B shares carry 50 votes each but no economic rights (no dividends or residual value). And effectively, this does concentrate control within those two institutions, which I believe should actually translate into a discount rather than a valuation premium. And when you couple that with a small public float, I think it helps explain why the stock has been so volatile since its IPO.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, NNNN currently trades at a $1.4 billion market cap, which is remarkably pricey given its fundamentals. Since NNNN is an FPI, that means we only get annual updates akin to a 10-K report from a regular US issuer. The latest 20-F from NNNN is now a bit outdated, as it was filed back in April 2025. However, we know that in December 2024, NNNN's balance sheet had $11.8 million in cash against no financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Its book value reached $17.2 million, which would now imply an extremely expensive P/B of 81.4.

Having said that, NNNN's IPO gave us a thin float of 1.6 million shares (Class A) and raised around $8.0 million in gross proceeds. So, if we add that to its book value, we could estimate an "updated" figure at around $25.2 million. However, that would hint at a pricey P/B of 55.6, which is still much higher compared to its peers' median P/B of just 2.9. Thus, I imagine the premium is largely tied to its PCR systems and newly announced Chikungunya rapid test. Their PCR products are indeed interesting, and the EuroMedLab, AVA, and ACMTT exhibitions announced a promising chemistry lineup for testing.

However, I believe the excitement is mostly around their potential with AP-100 Ultra-Fast PCR and Chikungunya. The story is simple: NNNN can soon offer a handheld PCR test that takes 15 minutes and gives results comparable to laboratories. In theory, this could definitely capture a sizeable market, though even that is difficult to quantify. Plus, Chikungunya outbreaks can definitely benefit from faster testing solutions. However, even at its COVID-19 heights, NNNN's revenues only reached $23.5 million in 2022. And I find it incredibly difficult to reach those sales again with Chikungunya alone, even if its outbreaks escalate in the coming years. Plus, their PCR testing announcements didn't include FDA approvals as far as I could find.

Therefore, for the most part, NNNN's products are CE-marked under the old IVDD, which allowed them to be CE-certified by manufacturer self-declaration. However, looking ahead, NNNN's ability to commercialize its products in the EU will require an upgrade to IVDR. And securing an updated IVDR will need a Notified Body (NB) review and a CE certificate. Basically, the EU intends to increase the bar in terms of clinical evidence and post-market surveillance. And NNNN stated in its 20-F form that it hasn't yet formalized an NB partnership and is updating technical files. In practice, this does create some timing risk given industry-wide NB involvement, and the IVDR transition deadlines go from 2027 to 2029.

Likewise, NNNN's US situation with its products remains in "pre-clearance." This means that its OTC use must have an FDA authorization to be widely commercialized. Until then, a physician's office can use NNNN's tests only if it holds a valid CLIA waiver. On top of that, NNNN's product adoption is largely payor-dependent. This is because using its testing products doesn't have automatic insurance coverage and reimbursement, so each payer decides whether or not it covers NNNN's tests on a case-by-case basis.

Lastly, if we include the $8.0 million raised from their IPO, I estimate NNNN's cash reached $19.8 million. And its 20-F disclosed NNNN generated $2.1 million in cash flows from operations. So, from that perspective, I would say NNNN seems relatively self-sustainable as long as they don't massively ramp up their CAPEX or R&D expenses going forward. But, even then, I would argue that's unlikely given their ambitions to scale manufacturing of their new Ultra-Fast PCR tests and meet regulatory requirements. Yet, until we get an update, we should assume they have enough financing for the near term.

Conclusion: Still Bearish

Overall, I have to say I remain skeptical about NNNN's prospects. Even if the bull case materializes, I doubt it will be enough to justify its already expensive price tag. That caps its upside potential even in the blue-sky scenario. And on the other hand, if NNNN fails to execute or faces regulatory setbacks, it could quickly translate into substantial shareholder losses. This is particularly true since its stock has a very thin float and trading volume. Thus, I believe it's fair to reiterate my "Sell" rating on NNNN at these levels.

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines: Record Free Cash Flow, Costs In Check, Q3 Earnings On Tap
data.description:
SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD) tallied record dollar volume on Thursday, Oct. 9. While the aggregate sum of shares traded was far below previous sessions, given the massive rally and record GLD price level, its dollars traded were never higher. Historically, such events have been relatively bearish for gold. But the yellow metal bounced back on the morning of Oct. 10.

Today, I’m revisiting Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM), the largest position in the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). Now an $81 billion market cap gold miner, I had a "Buy" rating on the stock in the second quarter. Shares are up 32% since then, though the stock endured its worst day in three months on the big dollar-volume down day for gold. I reiterate a "Buy" rating, but I’ll note some technical damage later in the article. The stock is also getting closer to what I now consider to be fair value.

Record GLD Dollar-Volume on Oct. 9, 2025

AEM: Biggest GDX Stock

Back in July, AEM reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q2 non-GAAP EPS of $1.94 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $1.80, while revenue of $2.82 billion was a massive 36% jump from the same period a year earlier and $120 million above consensus. At the time, the management team saw full-year gold production in the 3.3 to 3.5 million ounces of gold range, with all-in-sustaining costs (AISC) unchanged for the year between $1,250 and $1,300. With gold near $4,000, that means very wide margins.

Shares traded higher by just 0.8% in the session that followed, however. Of course, gold miners’ meteoric rally commenced in August and endured through the middle of this past week. Looking ahead to the Q3 report due out later this month, the options market prices in a high 4.8% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the Oct. 29 report.

Looking back on the quarter that was, AEM delivered impressive top-line numbers while keeping costs in check. Production numbers were also above expectations, and I thought the full-year guidance reiteration was somewhat conservative—we could see those figures get ratcheted up in the Q3 report, in my view. What’s encouraging to see within the gold-mining space is that most companies are keeping costs under control. Capital discipline is also a hallmark of the current cycle, unlike the bullish one 15 years ago.

AEM’s gold output of 866,000 ounces was above forecasts, and the AISC tallied $1,289 per ounce. Its Detour Lake, Malartic, Upper Beaver, and Hope Bay exploration endeavors appeared successful and generally on track for further production gains (the firm aims for over 1 million ounces of production from Detour and Malartic each). From a shareholder perspective, the company repurchased $100 million of stock, leading its peers in buybacks.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is considered very strong this year, and I suspect that we’ll see out-year estimates tick up materially given the steep gold price rise in the last few months. Moreover, with proven cost control, $4,000 gold should result in extremely high operating leverage, in which higher sales flow to the bottom line.

The sellside, meanwhile, has turned much more bullish on AEM, as evidenced by a high 15 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days compared to zero downgrades. Finally, AEM is highly profitable, as evidenced by a high $6.16 of free cash flow per share over the past 12 months (up from $4.67 at the time of my June assessment).

AEM: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates. If we assume $8 of normalized non-GAAP earnings per share (up from my previous assumption of $6, thanks to higher gold prices) and apply the same 22x multiple (slightly below its long-term average), then shares should trade near $176.

So, there’s still room to the upside, but the gap between the current stock price and my intrinsic value target has narrowed.

AEM: Still Attractive on Earnings, Low Yield, Modest PEG Ratio

Key risks for AEM include, increasingly, the boom/bust behavior of gold prices. Currently, heavy central bank buying appears to be a price-insensitive upside catalyst, but that could always reverse.

For AEM, it must focus on cost controls and operational safety at its mines. Along with gold price volatility, access to development financing is important. Analysts did question AEM’s growth durability at some of its mines during the Q2 call.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q3 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, Oct. 29, for AMC. Shares trade ex a $0.40 dividend on Monday, Dec. 1.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares not too far from what I consider to be fair value, AEM’s technical situation is mixed. There are some signs of exhaustion. Notice in the chart below that the RSI momentum oscillator has fallen from very lofty levels above 70 to the mid-50s. For now, with support near the $155 mark, I still see the bulls controlling the primary trend. Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, supporting the higher-trend thesis.

Also, take a look at the shorter-term 50dma. It, too, is rising, and I would not be surprised to see AEM tag that line in the weeks ahead. The $172 near-term peak could be a line in the sand that the bears will cling to. With the RSI giveback and a lack of volume by price until about $127, there is a bearish air pocket that could take this high flier lower in the near term. Having a limit to buy the stock at $155 appears like a sound strategy.

AEM: Shares Pare 2025 Gains, Eyeing the 50dma, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on AEM. With gold up by more than 50% on the year and up substantially since my June analysis, I raise my price target. The chart exhibits some cracking, but I expect dip-buyers to enter the scene. Investors should also not discount the chance of a bullish “performance chase” into the end of the year.

data.title: Costco: Cracks Are Starting To Appear
data.description:
The problem with Costco Wholesale Corporation (NASDAQ:COST) stock has been the valuation. Back in late June, I conducted an analysis and found solid fundamentals such as rebounding EPS growth and strong membership statistics. However, the P/E ratio was elevated, and that led to my sell rating. The stock is down around 7% since then, and so the valuation may be correcting now.

In the analysis below, it is shown that there may be some cracks appearing in Costco's fundamentals. Most recent results show that key metrics are not moving in the right direction, and furthermore, there are some concerns I have about the membership statistics. While they have new warehouses planned for FY2026, I would say that the near-term outlook for the business is unfavorable. Despite this, the valuation is still at a major premium to the Consumer Staples sector, leading me to reiterate my sell rating on the stock.

Revenue Growth Stable

Costco reported their FY2025 Q4 earnings in late September, and top-line results were quite robust. The company generated a total of $86.156 billion in revenues when including membership fees. That is an 8.1% increase YoY, and as you can see above, that is nearly unchanged from the previous quarter's growth rate. While a sub-10% increase is nothing exciting, note that Q4's top line was quite robust versus historic growth rates, especially considering consumer weakness headwinds. However, you'll see later that their various U.S. metrics trailed international counterparts. Lastly, Costco's top line beat estimates by $99.41 million, and so there was a good degree of overall outperformance in the quarter.

U.S. Underperforms

In adjusted comparable terms, there was a notable deterioration in key metrics for Costco's business. This suggests that consumer weakness effects may be catching up to the company. They reported total sales growth of 6.4%, a slowdown from Q3's 8% growth rate. In addition, note that the U.S. was a major drag on sales growth, and so at this point it seems that tariffs have had a worse effect on consumers in the U.S. than in international markets. That is also shown in the ticket price increases.

For the company as a whole, there was a 2.6% increase in ticket price, less than Q3's 2.7% increase, and so customers seem to be a bit hesitant to spend on high-price-tag items. Again, the U.S. was a drag on performance as another signal that U.S. consumers are trying to bargain hunt more than their international counterparts.

Lastly, the traffic increase of 3.7% is another slowdown from Q3's 5.2%. This time, 'Other International' markets underperformed the U.S. but still, overall, the trajectory of store traffic is less than ideal. As a whole, I would say these key business metrics show that Costco is beginning to feel the effects of weak sentiment. With the U.S. being their largest market by far, the relative underperformance is a concern moving forward in my view.

Other Potential Concerns

While membership income growth for Q4 accelerated from Q3 levels, that was mainly due to increases in prices. There are some potentially concerning signs regarding their other membership statistics. Overall membership growth and cardholder growth slowed as a sign of lower demand. However, perhaps even more worrying are the lower renewal rates both relative to Q3 and the prior year period. This signals lower satisfaction of customers at the new membership price point. Therefore, even though membership income has strengthened, Costco is potentially deterring customers with the higher costs.

Another separate concern is the slowdown in their online sales growth. With e-commerce becoming increasingly important in today's world, Costco's slowdown may be a step in the wrong direction. In Q4, adjusted e-commerce comparable sales were up by 13.5%. That is a notable deceleration from Q3's 15.7% growth rate. In fact, their online sales growth actually slowed more than their overall adjusted comparable sales did, and so it seems their e-commerce efforts have weakened. Site traffic growth actually accelerated, but nonetheless, the deterioration in sales growth is not what investors want to see.

Profitability Is Decent

Costco showed some profitability improvements, but not enough to be considered a major improvement. Gross margin was 11.13%, but it was only a 3 basis point expansion when excluding the effects of gasoline, and so cost control was only very mildly better. As for the company's operational efficiency, there was also a small improvement. SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue decreased by 9 basis points when excluding gas impacts. As a result, bottom-line growth did eclipse the pace of top-line growth by a slight amount. Their EPS was reported to be $5.87, beating expectations by $0.07. As you can see above, growth is a bit stagnant relative to historical levels, and so as a whole, I would say Costco is experiencing a period of moderate sluggishness.

Expansion To Continue

As discussed earlier, key metrics for Costco are not on a particularly good trajectory, but that isn't stopping the company from continuing to invest in future growth. As you can see in the stats above, from the end of FY2024 to the end of FY2025, they opened 24 net new warehouses, with 15 being in America. All markets saw new warehouses in FY2025, in FY2026 that will continue. Over the coming fiscal year, they are expecting to open 30 net new warehouses, with 20 being in America. Therefore, the weakness we have seen in their U.S. metrics is not deterring Costco from investing more in their key market. Overall, the continued ambitions for expansion are bold and set a tone that management remains confident in the long-term outlook for the business. Considering that consumer headwinds will only be temporary, this expansion may make sense for the long term.

Valuation Remains Excessive

Costco's forward P/E ratio has contracted quite a bit recently. However, I believe the current reading of 45.45 shows that the valuation is still too rich. Overall, I'm not overly impressed with the company's Q4 results. Key adjusted comparable metrics deteriorated by a notable amount, and there are some concerns I have about the deceleration in new memberships as well as the lower renewal rate. Despite their plans for more warehouse openings in FY2026, data suggest that the trajectory of demand is slowing. Therefore, while these new warehouses may be a good investment down the road, in the near term, they may not make too much sense.

Sure, the forward P/E ratio has contracted since the middle of this year, but at the same time, I'm also seeing some cracks emerging in their fundamentals that weren't there before. Note that the Consumer Staples sector only has a median non-GAAP forward P/E of 15.87 as of this writing, and so Costco still seems to be at a major premium while its fundamentals have grown shakier. Therefore, I would say the stock remains significantly overvalued at current levels.

Technical Analysis

Overall, the technicals for Costco stock are currently mixed. The 50-day SMA crossed below the 200-day SMA back in August for a death cross. Therefore, the long-term outlook now is mainly bearish. However, if you look closely at the MACD and RSI, you can see that both indicators made a higher low so far this month than back in late July. Meanwhile, the stock price is now lower than it was back in late July, and so there is some positive divergence currently. That could mean there is bullish reversal potential or at least a chance of some reprieve from the selling pressure.

For the near term, most signs are bearish. The 9-day EMA is below the 21-day EMA, and the stock is well below the Bollinger Bands midline. However, while the RSI also shows that the bears are in firm control, the MACD histogram suggests that bearish momentum is on the retreat. Therefore, traders should not rule out a near-term bounce at this point.

Concluding Remarks

Costco had long been a solid business selling at an elevated price. However, now I believe there are some signs that weakness is building for the company. For example, the deceleration in adjusted comparable sales growth and ticket price growth shows that the company is not immune to economic and consumer headwinds. Furthermore, the increased membership prices are seemingly denting renewal figures. Despite these potential new concerns, the stock continues to trade at a mighty premium, and so I don't find the stock to be particularly attractive at current levels. For that reason, I reiterate my sell rating.

data.title: Gold Rally Still Got Legs? Collect Big Income From GDXY
data.description:
Introduction

Gold (GLD) has performed exceptionally in recent years due to rising inflation, geopolitical issues, macro uncertainty, a declining U.S. dollar, and potential stagflation. I remember back in 2008/2009 during the Great Financial Crisis, older guys in the office were talking about buying and holding gold.

Only if I had listened then. But like Warren Buffett, I've never been too keen on holding something that doesn't pay me. Buffett has scoffed at the idea of gold as an investment. To paraphrase, he once said: It was a hunk of yellow metal and not a great inflation hedge.

However, gold has continued to rally and outperform. And I believe it does have its place inside a diversified portfolio. Add in covered call funds, and being able to collect income with investments like YieldMax Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:GDXY) makes it worthwhile.

While I do think there are better funds out there for reasons I explain in the article, the ETF has its place for strictly income-oriented investors.

In this article, I discuss GDXY's fundamentals and why income-focused investors may find this covered call ETF attractive.

How Far Will Gold Rally?

Gold has continued to perform strongly and recently touched above $4,000 an ounce, its 7th consecutive week of rallying. Investors may wonder if this will continue or if we're in for some sort of correction soon.

While no one should try to time the market, I suspect the rally could continue for the near term. Especially, as macro uncertainty remains.

At the time of writing, the U.S. government is still shut down with no end in sight. Although, I'm assuming we will likely see a deal in the near future. But gold could continue rallying as a result of more uncertainty.

You can see below, GLD has significantly outperformed SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), up over 52% compared to 18.55% and nearly 27%, respectively.

While a correction could be near, I think the rally could continue, at least for the near term. If so, covered call ETFs will continue to perform well and reward shareholders with big distributions. And this is where YieldMax's Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF comes in.

47.05% Distribution

I will say I've been critical of YieldMax's funds in past articles because of their NAV erosion. As I think there are much better firms out there when it comes to the covered call space. NEOS & Roundhill are two issuers that come to mind.

But GDXY's large distributions, and much cheaper share price in comparison to peers like NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI), FT Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (IGLD), and Kurv Gold Enhanced Income ETF (KGLD) stand out.

At the time of writing, GDXY has a distribution of 47.05%, much higher in comparison to its peer group. GDXY uses a synthetic options selling call and put options on VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX).

This means GDXY does not actually hold shares in GDX, but just uses it to create income. They also hold U.S. treasuries to generate income, similar to many of their peers. As a result, this caps their upside.

But the large distributions that they pay, GDXY is appealing to income-focused investors like myself. They have a relatively short track record with an inception date of May 20, 2024. So, almost a year and a half under its belt.

So far, the fund has performed well against IGLD. Since both IAUI and KGLD have inception dates of June and July of this year, their records were too short for a complete comparison. IGLD has the longest track record of the group, with an inception date of March 2, 2021.

Over the past year, GDXY has underperformed both the S&P (SP500) and IGLD in terms of price. Both are up double-digits, showing the former hasn't been able to capture as much upside as its peer IGLD.

But in the past 6 months, GDXY has performed well, slightly IGLD by less than 1%.

In total returns, GDXY really shines, as their large distributions have led to a total return of 54.67%, nearly doubling the performance of IGLD.

And in the past year, up over 57% compared to nearly 38.42% for IGLD.

And this is because GDXY has seen strong NAV growth in the past 6 months, up 38.16%.

Because GDXY is actively managed, the fund does have the highest expense ratio among its peer group at 0.99%. This is with the exception of KGLD's 1.0%. NEOS', a favorite among income-investors, expense ratio is the lowest at 0.78%. IGLD's expense ratio is 0.85%.

While I personally like those with lower expense ratios, below 1% is reasonable. Especially, if the fund pays large distributions. Another benefit of GDXY is that most of their distributions are considered return of capital, making them suitable for younger investors.

As a result of taxes being deferred, investors can hold GDXY inside a taxable account. This eliminates the need for a tax-advantaged account like a Roth and avoids not being able to withdraw until age 59 1/2.

Here's the distributions GDXY has paid so far in 2025. Although distributions vary, the ETF has paid an average of $0.5562 so far, with their last distribution amounting to nearly $0.65 a share.

IGLD has paid an average of $0.13. IAUI's last distribution was $0.5434, while KGLD has paid two distributions of $0.25. I think over time their peers will likely remain more consistent, making them better holdings for the long-term. This is especially true regarding IAUI.

But if you're currently seeking income, GDXY is the more attractive option at the moment to continue riding the gold wave.

Risks & Conclusion

With GDXY being a fund of YieldMax, known for consistent NAV erosion with their funds, they are likely to see more downside should gold experience a correction. As a result, their distributions will likely remain volatile and less consistent in comparison to peers.

However, they do sell put options against GDX to mitigate downside. But a severe correction will result in underperformance.

I do think there are better peers than GDXY due to potential NAV erosion, limited upside, and their higher expense ratio. But they're an attractive way to ride the gold rally should it continue. You also get to see some capital appreciation while collecting a large yield.

data.title: Applied Digital: In AI Dollar We Trust (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ:APLD) is one of the loudest (and fastest growing) plays within the world of AI infrastructure build-out, and its earnings report for Q1 (reported after the bell on Thursday) just pushed the stock about 25% higher in pre-market.

These days, everything is about compute capacity: who wants more of it, who has the capacity, and which of the two partners is up. Applied Digital is a winning chip within the gigawatt wars, with total anticipated contracted lease revenue adding up to $11B. About $7B of this $11B figure is from the CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) deal for two 15-year leases for 250MW at the Ellendale, North Dakota campus, Polaris Forge 1 sites. CoreWeave has expanded the lease to cover the full 400 MW of capacity of Polaris Forge 1.

So, the latest positive for Applied Digital was a new lease agreement with CoreWeave for another 150 MW capacity at Applied Digital’s Polaris Forge 1 campus in North Dakota. The deal basically brings the entire site to full capacity, meaning this is a better and faster-than-expected utilization rate, which bodes extremely well for the Polaris Forge 2 campus. In our opinion, Polaris Forge 2 is the reason to buy the stock. This campus is under construction, but it adds another 300 MW worth of capacity to be leased out and has the potential to scale to 1GW. This second site is expected to go online in 2026 and hit full capacity in 2027. The fact that Applied Digital is building out more capacity is a bullish signal for sure.

The earnings results compel us to reassess our bearish sentiment on Applied Digital. The stock is up almost 30% since our sell rating back in late September (this is without counting the pre-market gains that have taken the stock price from $29.29 per share at Thursday's close to $37.04 in pre-market trading).

This looks bad because it is -

Here was our sell-rating logic: the online capacity of ~400 MW is maxed out, and it's a waiting game to bring the rest online without any execution issues. Our concern with the stock was that it is priced for perfection, leaving little room for any slip-up, competition, or any moderation in spending on AI data center capacity. This earnings call proved us wrong: there is more capacity set to come online pretty soon (next year is now less than three months away). This could expand the capacity Applied Digital has under its belt and bring in more contract wins. So, we're upgrading the stock to a buy.

1Q26 Earnings recap

For Q1, Applied Digital reported revenue growth of 84% from a year ago to $64.2M, blowing analyst estimates for $50M out of the water. If you had to narrow the market reaction down to one data point, this would be it (plus the new site under construction). The company brought in an extra $14.2M more than expected, with the major driver being its data center hosting business, which generated $37.9M for the quarter, which makes up 59% of total revenue.

The other piece of news is that Applied Digital isn’t compromising on cost control to achieve higher revenue: the adjusted loss per share was $0.3, which is smaller than the anticipated $0.13 loss. Cost of revenue is naturally expanding; it was up 144% to $55.6M (of which $25M was due to infrastructure build for HPC host agreements). Operating margin was -34.7%, lower than the -24.2% reported in the year-ago quarter. The company is securing more project financing: $5B preferred equity facility with Macquarie Asset Management, which should help with the construction of the Polaris Forge 2.

Management is hitching its growth to AI and the hyper-spend we’re seeing from the tier 1 players, with AI CapEx spend from the top four players alone coming up to $330B for the current year. Applied Digital CEO, Wes Cummins, said the following about the current AI backdrop:

With hyperscalers expected to invest approximately $350B into AI deployment this year, we believe we are in a prime position to serve as the modern-day picks and shovels of the intelligence era.

Part of the post-earnings conversation is whether the revenue beat is due to a one-time contribution from CoreWeave. We believe that the CoreWeave positives are more than well-baked into the stock price. So, now it’s all about how much more capacity is going to come online and who is in line to scoop up that extra capacity. The current estimate is that over the next three to five years, the Polaris Forge 1 and 2 sites can “scale to 1GW each and up to 4GW of long-term potential capacity.” Management is eyeing a net operating income run rate of $1B within five years, which convinces us of the growth trajectory with the Polaris Forge 2 site next year.

Bottom line: valuation, stock & outlook

Applied Digital is fully bathed in the AI story, so it’s no surprise that it trades at a premium. On a forward EV/Sales, the stock trades at 34.08x versus a group average of 3.54x. This is a jump scare at first until you take a look at the other names with similar exposure, which we attempt to do in the chart below, showcasing the three-month EV/Sales. Indeed, Applied Digital’s valuation is getting more expensive (and continues to face profitability challenges), but it does not look all too out of place when put in the context of its direct peer group.

The six-month chart below also shows the hyper runs these players have been experiencing with the wave of AI infrastructure buildout. IREN Limited (IREN), in specific, which we’ve been bullish on, is outperforming the peer group up 982%, followed by Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) up 465% with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) deal and stellar earnings report to thank, and then Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) up 430%. CoreWeave actually comes in last, up 193%.

Valuation and even a stock price run-up are not sufficient to make calls within the current market. RSI is through the roof currently at 89, so we're expecting some cooling off after this rally. We recommend investors watch the stock for entry points when the stock exists in overbought territory.

What matters to us is the potential tailwinds moving forward. The fact that we’re seeing management execute on the build-out of more AI factories opens an opportunity for new lease agreements. Considering Applied Digital has gotten the nod of confidence and validation from Nvidia’s sweetheart CoreWeave, we’re more optimistic about more contract wins for the company in the new era of AI spend. We're upgrading the stock to a buy for the mid-term.

data.title: Ferrari: Your Fear Is My Profit (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Ferrari N.V. (NYSE:RACE) analysis, in which I issued a Hold rating, the stock has lost -16% in price.

On October 9, 2025, Ferrari held a Capital Markets Day and released a new mid- and long-term strategic plan and updated their 2025 guidance. While 2025 guidance revealed a modest raise, the 2030 targets fell short of what the market was expecting. Ferrari's 2030 revenue target of €9B implies a ~5% CAGR from 2025 levels, which is decent but not aggressive for a luxury brand with strong pricing power and brand equity. Also, Ferrari revised its earlier electric-vehicle ambitions. Instead of having 40% of its models fully electric by 2030, management is now aiming for only 20%. For the purposes of this analysis, I will largely negate the electric vehicle analysis and focus on my analysis of management's financial/operational strategy in the macro.

Underpromising To Overdeliver

I saw the price down 16%, and I bought 6% of my NAV. Instinct often drives the best investors, and I take a leaf out of Druckenmiller's book: "Invest, then research."

Firstly, I believe that management is underpromising to overdeliver (the company has the reputation and track record for this). Ferrari caps volumes and sells into a pre-booked order book, which gives above-average earnings visibility. Also, mix and personalization, rather than units, drive earnings beats. Management has an explicit track record of setting conservative full-year ranges, and then it raises guidance around Q2–Q3 once mix and costs are de-risked. A similar outcome is likely to materialize here over a longer horizon. For evidence, consider that all 2024 targets were exceeded. In essence, I think that management's 2030 guidance is essentially a floor, not a ceiling. The market appears to be reading it too negatively, which may be what management wants for stable equity growth over the long term (taking a hit now for smoother upside over time).

Secondly, I believe management is not as focused on revenue growth as the market would like, instead focusing on margin accretion through brand-equity factors. Indeed, CEO Benedetto Vigna told investors that the company "must manage scarcity" to succeed. This likely reflects a focus on protecting pricing power and mix in a logical place instead of chasing volume. Limited editions, deeper personalization, and opex adjustments could all still drive compound annual growth. Even though the operating profit guidance through 2030 was also revised down, I believe this is the area management is most likely to outperform in. Their guidance reflects conservatism, managing expectations, and a commitment to stability and preservation over obtuse growth. That makes Ferrari a defensive value investment after the recent post-capital-markets-day de-rating.

I think that we could realistically see a 25% return from RACE stock in about 9 months. This hits my 30% annualized CAGR target cleanly if the bull case materializes. Let me run you through the math. Management actually raised 2025 guidance, so we are right to assume that near-term growth remains intact. We can still be conservative and revise the December 2027 consensus normalized EPS down from $13.50 to $13 for security. The forward P/E non-GAAP ratio had stabilized recently around 45, but this will logically be de-rated for two reasons: 1) sentiment shift from conservative guidance and 2) slower EPS growth from 2028 onwards due to saturation. For ultra conservatism, we can use a multiple of 40 (a standard base-case assumption for a luxury stock in the league of Ferrari, given its track record and historical sentiment). The recent pullback has drawn the forward P/E to 39 already, but I expect some reversion to 42 over the next 6 months and stabilization around 40 by December 2026 on a normalized basis. This places my December 2026 price target at $520. As I bought at approximately $405 per share, the implied upside is 28.4%.

We can also invest confidently here because the return framework is asymmetric. Yes growth is "slower" than expected based on new guidance, but the stock will not realistically draw down over many years or months based on weak long-term guidance if brand strength and growth durability remain intact. Ferrari is a stable compounder, nothing has changed about this with the new guidance.

The asymmetry is also clear in the technical charts. The 14-day RSI is well below 30. I am so pleased that I bought at this deep red point as it really shows an excellent, rare, and optimal entry point that is so hard to strike at as one can't monitor every stock at every moment of the day. This is a perfect moment to be greedy when others are fearful.

I'm setting a take-profit target of $500 per share to see if this is executed within the next 9 months, which would be about a 25% return in 3/4 of a year. If it doesn't execute automatically I will probably just hold RACE long-term.

The Bear Case

If Ferrari stock fails to achieve resistance to multiple compressions, a forward P/E of ~35 could materialize in December 2026 on 2027 EPS of $12.80 due to operational failures like low uptake of electric vehicles ("Elettrica"). Still, at this valuation and EPS outcome, the stock price will be $450 by December 2026, which is still a 12.5% return in about 12 months from today. So, we are really looking at a 10% return in 9 months in the case of "failure" of my investment thesis and a 25% return in 9 months in the case of "success." When incorporated into a diversified portfolio full of high-alpha compounders, a cash position, and some tactical trades, RACE fits at my 6% of NAV sizing.

The CEO's focus on scarcity caps throughput. ASP expansion is also slowing as the hybrid mix stabilizes. Scarcity does support pricing power, but it will limit operating leverage through lower access to fresh top-line capital. Management will have to focus on opex here to boost shareholder satisfaction, but there is some concern that the brand is looking to be "retained" rather than shareholders being rewarded. Even in this instance, capital is parked safely at a decent valuation with RACE stock right now, and the thesis can be reviewed monthly or quarterly to see if management is indeed focusing on shareholder rewards or has become too focused on a closed sense of brand preservation that ignores the innate interplay of market returns with customer satisfaction. There are obvious positive feedback loops on brand strength from high ROIC over time (brands don't thrive in preservation mode; they thrive in customer-centric and targeted growth).

That said, I think any bearish analysts on RACE stock right now will be significantly ignoring the stability of the brand and the durability of the premium valuation. Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is an extreme example of a company that can sustain a premium valuation without the requisite growth in real-time, and Ferrari's situation, while different, is tangential in being a luxury brand priced for premium durability. In Tesla's case, it is a premium brand priced for narrative optionality.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Ferrari's recent pullback is an asymmetric investment opportunity supported by structural brand resilience. Management's conservative 2030 targets indicate underpromising for reliable over-deliverance later; there are no outright signs of structural weakness, just a cautious operational strategy to retain brand strength, which is exactly what long-term RACE investors would want. Guidance has even been raised for 2025, and the sub-40 P/E ratio at the moment is opportune as an entry point, given minimal likelihood of further valuation de-rating and a high likelihood of the stock regaining strength as sentiment stabilizes and management works to support this.

Setting a take-profit of $500 per share feels wise to me, as I expect that this will be realistically achieved within 9 months, and upside beyond this will be capped by moderate growth prospects. Ferrari works best as a value trade for elite alpha seekers or as a long-term holding for moderate alpha expectations. My trade outcome is expected to be 25% in 9 months.

data.title: DGRO: 10-Year Dividend Growth Streak Likely To Continue
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:DGRO) on March 11, 2004, when I rated it a "hold" and cautioned DGI investors that the days of double-digit dividend growth were likely in the past. Since that review was published, DGRO has delivered a 24.48% total return, but it increased dividends per share by only 0.33% over the last year, as shown below.

Lower dividend growth rates weren't unique to DGRO—earnings growth rates just didn't keep up to justify them, especially for the value-oriented stocks it holds. Still, DGRO is one of only five large-cap value ETFs (and one of 12 ETFs across all categories) to increase dividends for ten consecutive years, and along with its low 0.08% expense ratio, it's no wonder DGRO has attracted nearly $35 billion in assets since its launch on June 10, 2024.

I have two purposes today. The first is to re-evaluate the likelihood of DGRO's ten-year dividend growth streak continuing, and the second is to explore whether any of the other four large-cap value ETFs with 10+ year dividend growth streaks (VTV, VYM, VIG, SCHD) are better buys. With that said, let's dive right in, and as always, I look forward to answering your questions in the comments section afterward.

DGRO Strategy Overview

As I mentioned, DGRO has nearly $35 billion in assets under management and a low 0.08% expense ratio. It also yields 2.02%, has a 6.68% three-year dividend growth rate, and is joined by VTV, VYM, VIG, and SCHD as the only other large-cap value ETFs on 10+ year dividend growth streaks.

DGRO's 10-year streak is no accident. It tracks the Morningstar US Dividend Growth Index, which covers about the top 97% of the investable U.S. stock market. Eligible securities must have at least five years of consecutive dividend growth, a positive earnings consensus forecast, payout ratios below 75%, and not be in the top 10% by dividend yield, as summarized below.

DGRO's 5Y Dividend Growth Screen Is Right

The years of consecutive dividend growth screen is the most consequential, and to illustrate, consider the following average three-year dividend growth rates for the roughly 1,200 dividend-paying stocks (excluding REITs) with market caps above $1B:

  • 0-4 Years (693 stocks): 6.23%.
  • 5-9 Years (135 stocks): 9.87%.
  • 10-14 Years (177 stocks): 9.41%.
  • 15-19 Years (67 stocks): 7.51%.
  • 20-24 Years (47 stocks): 7.29%.
  • 25+ Years (100 stocks): 6.14%.

This data illustrates how 5-15 years of consecutive dividend growth is right in the sweet spot, and with 312 securities in these two buckets (135 + 177), DGI investors should want them in their portfolios. Furthermore, when factoring in the positive earnings forecast and 75% dividend payout ratio screens, the statistics look even better for the shorter-streak stocks.

  • 0-4 Years (558 stocks): 7.12%.
  • 5-9 Years (124 stocks): 10.21%.
  • 10-14 Years (168 stocks): 9.39%.
  • 15-19 Years (59 stocks): 7.95%.
  • 20-24 Years (43 stocks): 7.19%.
  • 25+ Years (92 stocks): 6.50%.

It's nice to confirm that the strategy has logic. Each screen serves a purpose to improve the chances of dividend growth, and importantly, its dividend growth is organic. In other words, its components are doing the heavy lifting, and DGRO holders aren't seeing their dividends increase simply because it yield-weights its components each quarter or systematically excludes low-yielding stocks each year, which is how SCHD operates.

DGRO Performance Summary

Since July 1, 2014, DGRO has gained an annualized 11.72% compared to 10.42%, 10.11%, and 10.23% for VTV, VYM, and SCHD, respectively. It's also slightly outpaced VIG by 0.08% per year—not bad for a higher-yielding fund in a mostly bullish market (2.02% vs. 1.63% trailing yield).

All five ETFs have similar risk statistics, including an annualized standard deviation figure of around 13-15% and maximum drawdown figures between 20-25%. However, consider that income on a $10,000 investment in January 2015 would grow from $246 to $654 from 2015 to 2014, equating to an 11.47% annualized dividend growth rate. Notably, the annualized growth rates for VTV and VYM were lower at 9.84% and 8.94%, so based on this nine-year growth rate, DGRO was the better choice for DGI investors. A 6.54% yield on cost over nine years is also probably sufficient for most people.

As for VIG, its annual income increased from $226 to $502, which works out to 9.27% per year, 2.20% short of DGRO's. However, SCHD's dividend growth was massive at 14.87%, and in a nutshell, this chart is why so many investors get excited about owning SCHD.

However, there are consequences to such high dividend growth because when earnings growth isn't there to support it, the market has a way of "making things right" by limiting share price gains. We've seen that in 2023, 2024, and 2025, with DGRO outperforming SCHD by 5.92%, 4.96%, and 11.29%.

DGRO Fundamental Analysis

A company must increase earnings to justify dividend growth, so since DGRO's strategy centers around dividend growth, earnings growth is non-negotiable. If companies try to increase dividends even when earnings are flat or falling, market participants might treat that as "robbing Peter to pay Paul", so to speak, and limit share price gains. Keep that in mind when analyzing the three-year historical earnings and dividend growth rates for DGRO and its peers in the table below.

Here are two takeaways to consider:

#1: DGRO's 1Y Historical / Estimated Earnings Growth Is Encouraging

The table reveals that DGRO's one-year historical and estimated earnings per share growth rates are 11.50% and 9.31% on a weighted average basis. Those rates are about the same as VTV and VYM and a few points behind VIG, but the bottom line is that they're all above the 7.92% three-year dividend growth rate of its current constituents. That's crucial to maintain because otherwise, payout ratios get stretched and investor confidence erodes. In my view, that's why SCHD has underperformed, as its current holdings have increased dividends despite seeing their earnings decline.

Still, DGRO's 4.32% three-year historical earnings growth rate is below its 7.92% dividend growth rate, so I remind readers that the double-digit dividend growth rates we saw earlier probably aren't likely, even though the odds of its 10-year dividend growth continuing are high.

#2: DGRO's P/E Ratios Represent Pretty Good Value For The Price

DGRO trades at 19.00x forward earnings, which, when considered alongside its 9.31% one-year estimated growth rate, indicates it offers pretty good value for its price. Consider the following modified forward PEG ratios for each ETF:

  • DGRO: 19.00x / 9.31% = 2.04x.
  • VTV: 18.11x / 8.06% = 2.25x.
  • VYM: 17.61x / 8.97% = 1.96x.
  • VIG: 22.86x / 11.90% = 1.92x.
  • SCHD: 14.30x / 2.20% = 6.50x.

As shown, DGRO is competitive from a GARP perspective, and when I expand this comparison to the entire large-cap value ETF category, DGRO ranks right in the middle at #66/119. In addition, DGRO ranks #50/119 when using its trailing P/E and one-year historical earnings growth rates, so I'd say DGRO is about average on these metrics. Certainly, there aren't any big red flags, and given its strong 18.83% net margins, I'd also say it's a reasonably high-quality dividend growth fund that's on solid footing at the moment.

Investment Recommendation

I'm more confident that DGRO's 10-year dividend growth streak will continue compared to last March. Its components' roughly 10% historical and estimated earnings growth makes future increases likely, and I like that earnings growth is trending positive again. Furthermore, I verified that each of DGRO's screens promotes dividend growth and that its five-year dividend growth requirement hits the sweet spot. Surprising as that may be to some, that's an important advantage it holds over Dividend Aristocrat ETFs like NOBL and SDY.

DGRO ranks close to average from a GARP perspective, and I think investors should consider higher-growth funds like VIG, which has a little extra buffer room available to continue its 11-year streak. The downside is a lower starting yield, but if your time horizon is long enough, I think there's a good chance you'll come out ahead on total returns and yield on cost.

I hope you enjoyed this review, and as always, please feel free to leave a comment or ask a question below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: PBF Energy: Regulatory Relief Pushes Shares Towards Fair Value
data.description:
Shares of PBF Energy Inc. (NYSE:PBF) have been a poor performer this year, losing nearly 10%. Its financial results have been strained by a weak refining environment over the winter, though that has improved. Additionally, it suffered a devastating fire at a refinery earlier this year, and recovery is still underway, though most losses are covered by insurance. That said, the stock has been recovering as refining sentiment has been improving. I last covered shares in August, rating the stock a “Strong Buy,” and this recommendation has played out with the stock adding 35%. Given how much it has run and new developments, now is a good time to revisit PBF.

I believe much of this rally is tied to increased optimism for the sector amid significant pro-industry regulatory actions. In late August, the Trump Administration’s EPA acted on 175 small refinery exemption (“SRE”) petitions, granting 63 in full and 77 partially, a favorable outcome for the industry. Some of these petitions had been pending for over five years, so this clears a significant legal overhang. I also expect an easier and quicker approach to exemptions going forward as well.

Now, in general, a refinery needs less than 75,000 bpd of throughput to qualify for an SRE. Each of PBF’s refineries is above this threshold, so I do not see the company being as much of a beneficiary of the EPA actions as companies like Delek (DK) and CVR Energy (CVI).

However, PBF is still likely to be a beneficiary of this action indirectly, in my view. When a refinery does not use enough renewable feedstock, it needs to purchase “RINs” to offset this shortfall. This can be a large expense for many companies, and PBF has significant RINs exposure. Last year, PBF’s RIN expense was over $500 million, but two years prior, it was $1.2 billion.

RINs pricing can be very volatile, and RINs have become increasingly expensive this year. PBF’s cost “essentially doubled” last quarter. As of Q2, PBF Energy has ~$520 million of accrued RINS obligations. Given the company has just a ~$3.5 billion market capitalization, this is a significant sum. Historically, smaller refiners have been large net buyers of RINs. With their RIN needs going down, their purchases will decline. Plus, much of their historic liability has been wiped away. For instance, CVI needs to buy 300 million fewer RINs.

With these refineries needing to buy fewer RINs, RINs pricing may fall back down, which in turn could reduce PBF’s operating costs. I see PBF free cash flow potentially benefitting by $50-$75 million per year from the EPA’s actions on SRE. At a 10x incremental free cash flow multiple, that is worth about $4-$5 a share, which explains quite a bit of PBF’s rally. This stronger run-rate free cash flow will also ease PBF’s debt concerns, which had been weighing on the stock.

Interestingly, while the stock has done very well, the refining environment has actually worsened a little bit of late. As you can see below, refining margins are much wider today than they were a year ago, but have fallen recently. Last year, the market was struggling with tight crude oil supplies and very strong refining utilization, and these dynamics have changed. Most notably, OPEC+ has been increasing oil production, which has increased the supply of crude oil relative to refined products like gasoline. This dynamic change should keep spreads wider than they were 6-12 months ago.

That said, margins have also fallen in recent weeks after highs hit over the early summer. One reason for this is that the United States has had a very quiet hurricane season. Typically, Gulf Coast refiners have to temporarily shut down some production for storms during the summer, but with no hurricanes hitting the U.S. mainland, refinery throughput has remained strong. Additionally, with sluggish economic growth, demand for gasoline does remain muted, and these factors may be limiting how wide crack spreads can stay. Still, I view levels north of $20-$22 as healthy and supportive of mid-cycle profitability, even if margins are not as strong as they once were.

With gasoline inventories relatively low, I do not see crack spreads crashing below $20 like they did last year, which should enable PBF to report sequentially solid results in Q3 and meaningfully better results over the winter than the company did last year.

There has also been important company-specific news for PBF Energy. On Sept. 30, PBF closed its previously announced sale of terminal assets, including 1.9 million barrels of capacity. This will generate about $175 million in cash proceeds, helping to assist with debt reduction.

In August, the company announced it will be promoting its head of treasury to be its new CFO. While the current CFO is retiring, Karen Davis will rejoin the company’s board of directors. This speaks to the fact that this is an amicable and smooth transition, and as a result, I do not expect there to be any change in the company’s capital allocation policy. The company is also in the midst of a $230 million cost-cutting program, though I remain highly cautious that PBF will be able to lower costs that much on a sustainable basis.

These actions should help to restore its balance sheet. So far this year, its debt load has increased by $900 million. PBF has $591 million of cash and $2.4 billion of debt. Thus far this year, it has faced $250 million of working capital headwinds as it deals with the shutdown of its Martinez refinery. I expect to see some recovery in working capital in H2. Combined with underlying cash flow, insurance payments, and the terminal proceeds, we should see net debt begin to recover by the end of the year. Ultimately, I would like to see PBF operate in the 10%-20% zone. Instead, the company ended Q2 with about 30% net debt to capital.

With their rally, shares are now in the $27-30 fair value range, which I have been using in recent months to value PBF, reflecting about $800-900 million of run-rate EBITDA. I do see the company likely running towards the higher end of my estimate over the medium term, given lower RINs costs. This should ultimately support at least $300 million of free cash flow, or about $30 at an ~8% free cash flow yield, reflecting the low growth and capital-intensive nature of the refining business.

Given how much the stock has run and its smaller RINs benefit, alongside some need for further debt reduction, I feel that PBF’s 35% gain has played out my "Strong Buy" thesis. As such, I am downgrading shares to a “Hold.” This has been an excellent run over just two months, and I would not buy here. I do not see the stock as overpriced, and so I see no rush to sell, though I would never object to investors looking to take a profit after such a large gain.

data.title: SMH: Likely To Keep Outperforming The Market And Move Higher
data.description:
The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) has had a phenomenal year, as it has appreciated by roughly 42%, which has outpaced the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 by a country mile. I don’t think it will come as a surprise to anyone that semiconductors have ripped higher throughout the year as the allocation of CapEx continues to increase toward building out data centers. Some of the largest benefactors have been NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO), along with Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and Micron (MU), as the entire stack has seen increased revenues and profitability. There have been many recent developments from Oracle (ORCL) announcing they will be raising $15 billion from a corporate bond sale as they expand their AI data center footprint, and from NVDA, who recently indicated that they plan on investing up to $100 billion in OpenAI to build and deploy 10 gigawatts of AI data centers. While the bears are still trying to convince the investment community that AI is in a bubble similar to the dot-com era in 1999, the reality is that the largest companies in the world are leading the charge, unlike in 1999, when start-ups were fueled by debt from venture capital without generating revenue. I think that the SMH is an interesting pick that provides investors with exposure across the semiconductor space, which will continue to outperform the market indices for the foreseeable future. I’m bullish on SMH and think the total addressable market (TAM) for data centers is only going to expand.

Following up on my previous article about SMH

Since my previous article was published in the middle of June (can be read here), shares of SMH have appreciated by 31.37%, while the S&P 500 has climbed 11.97%. I had discussed that the AI revolution and global chip demand made semiconductors a long-term growth story that would cause shares of SMH to move higher. The massive CapEx spend from big tech, in addition to geopolitical support like the CHIPS Act, is fueling industry expansion, which benefits all companies in the semiconductor value chain, from chips to fabrication. I had discussed that the semiconductor market was estimated to reach $2 trillion in 2032, and this could expand as more information is announced. I am following up with a new article to discuss what has happened since June and why I believe the current upward momentum will continue for SMH. I am bullish on AI and how the semiconductor industry will be impacted over the next several years.

An overview of the VanEck Semiconductor ETF

SMH is an ETF that takes a passive investment approach to building out a portfolio of companies throughout the semiconductor value chain. SMH will allocate at least 80% of its assets to semiconductor companies that range from U.S.-based companies to foreign entities. These companies may include medium-capitalization companies and foreign companies listed on a U.S. exchange. One of the major aspects to keep in mind is that holdings in SMH need to generate at least 50% of its revenues from semiconductors, which means that the fund will be heavy in semiconductors and semiconductor equipment. SMH doesn’t take an active management approach, so it could experience a lot of volatility with the market in the future.

SMH has a 100% weighting toward the technology sector, and the combination of NVDA and TSM makes up 28.53% of the ETF. When investors allocate capital toward SMH, they are effectively indicating that the demand for compute is going to increase and drive more revenue and profitability throughout the chip designers and manufacturers. SMH has $34.46 billion in assets under management (AUM), and investors are getting exposure to equipment suppliers, chip designers, and foundries. SMH is a single-focus ETF, but if the current velocity of spending continues, it may continue to be very lucrative for investors, as there could be enhanced levels of earnings expansion happening over the next several years for the positions within its portfolio.

Why I am still bullish on SMH after a 31.37% return since my previous article

I believe that we’re still in the early stages of the data center expansion due to harnessing the power of AI. In the 20th century, oil was the growth engine for the global economy, and while traditional sources of energy are still important, I believe that semiconductors are the foundational resource of our digital world, as they power everything from cloud data centers to the smartphones in our pockets. I don’t see any indication that technology has ever stood still, as every form of technology has evolved over time. I believe that SMH allows investors to capitalize on the industry leaders at the forefront of this global transformation regarding AI. The rise of Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) has created an unprecedented demand for high-performance Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and custom AI accelerators. We haven’t even started embedding full self-driving (FSD) or humanoid robotics into commercial use, which could enhance the rate at which data centers are being built to keep up with the future demand for inference training.

There has been a tremendous amount of news over the past several weeks that compounds what we learned from the Q2 conference calls. Microsoft (MSFT) recently delivered a data center announcement, where they are going to spend $4 billion and claim that it will be the most advanced in the world. After that, OpenAI and NVDA announced a strategic partnership to deploy 10 gigawatts of NVDA systems and buy directly from NVDA rather than sourcing their compute from 3rd parties. OpenAI also announced a deal with AMD where they would deploy 6 gigawatts of their next-generation chips starting in the 2nd half of 2026. Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META), and MSFT finished Q2 having spent $291.35 billion on CapEx over the trailing 12 months (TTM). In Q1 2025, the combined total of these companies CapEx allocation grew 62.35% ($27.61 billion) YoY. In Q2 it accelerated to 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. The rate that the big 4 are spending is increasing, and now OpenAI is becoming a self-sufficient player as they will build and operate their own data centers in addition to purchasing compute from other providers.

The SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) has a modified equal-weighting strategy that tracks the S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index. This approach gives smaller companies a more significant weighting than in a market cap-weighted fund such as SMH. The Invesco Semiconductor ETF (PSI) uses a quantitative methodology to select and weight its holdings, which is a smart beta approach that goes beyond market cap. This can take factors such as momentum and earnings expansion into consideration. The primary difference for me between SMH, XSD, and PSI is their portfolio construction. SMH focuses on the most liquid semiconductor companies, which leads to a concentrated portfolio towards industry juggernauts. While XSD offers a more diversified approach through its equal weight methodology and PSI takes factors like momentum into consideration, I believe that gaining excess exposure to companies such as NVDA, AMD, and TSM is the better strategy, as these are the companies that should benefit the most from the current infrastructure buildout that is occurring. History has shown us that SMH has dramatically outperformed SPY, QQQ, PSI, and XSD over the past 5 years, and I believe this will continue to occur.

When I look at the top 10 holdings for SMH, which account for 74.09% of the ETF, I get very bullish on the future. The combination of these companies is trading at 63.07 times 2025 earnings and is expected to grow their EPS on average by 126.88%, putting their valuation on 2027 earnings at 24.29 times. If I remove Intel Corporation (INTC) as it’s a massive outlier, the top 9 companies are trading at 35.08 times 2025 earnings and are expected to trade at 23.33 times 2027 earnings as they have an average of 45.61% EPS growth on the horizon. I actually think these EPS estimates could be significantly higher in the coming quarters as the analyst community continues to take their estimates higher after each earnings season. I believe that with all of the news that has been reported, there is a clear path for these companies to become more profitable and take shares of SMH higher.

The risks to investing in SMH

Technology continues to be a winning trade, and companies such as NVDA continue to make all-time highs, but there are risks to investing in SMH. The first risk is the lack of diversification, as SMH needs the CapEx spend to continue at this rate. If spending on data centers slows down and the semiconductor trade cools off, there is no broader market exposure through SMH to fall back on. This could cause shares of SMH to retrace more than the market in the event of a correction. The semiconductor industry is also vulnerable to geopolitical tensions, particularly U.S.-China relations, as its holdings face risks from trade restrictions, tariffs, or regional instability. Everyone should do their own due diligence on the semiconductor industry because investing in SMH is the equivalent of placing all of your chips on black at the roulette table.

Conclusion

The news keeps pouring in, and the United States has approved billions of dollars worth of NVDA chips to be exported through a bilateral AI deal with the UAE. Q3 earnings around the corner, and I believe that it will be bullish for semiconductors because GOOGL, AMZN, and META are likely to announce new CapEx spending plans to compete with OpenAI and MSFT. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) has highlighted that data centers are shaping commercial demand for energy, which is now projected to grow faster than other sectors due to their 24/7 load profiles. I think that this is an indication that the buildout cycle is nowhere near over, and shares of SMH allow investors to gain exposure to a basket of companies in the semiconductor industry that will stand to benefit from the continued data center growth. I think SMH will continue to exceed the market returns, especially when companies such as NVDA receive huge price target increases from $240 to $300.

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: A Discounted Valuation Meets Breakthrough Pipeline
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SRPT) is an interesting case. In recent times, the stock price has been under a lot of scrutiny from the unwanted results of some of its clinical trials. Over the past year, the stock has been down 80% hitting lows in the mid-$10 range. We saw a couple of patient deaths as a result of acute liver failure, which has knocked the stock down in big chunks over 2025. However, after hitting those lows back in late July, the stock has somewhat started to recover, reaching the $23 range we see today. I think this is the start of a turnaround for Sarepta, as they have a couple of nice therapy candidates progressing in their pipeline. The company’s near-term growth should be fueled by SRP-9003. It's a transformative gene therapy for Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), which, if successful later down the line, should expand Sarepta’s footprint beyond just Duchenne muscular dystrophy. It’s another rare-disease market with a sizable premium pricing opportunity similar to what they already show. The valuation on Sarepta also remains rather attractive given its $800 million cash figure. Data on the upcoming SRP-9003 study should fall sometime soon, and with a few points to look out for, a positive result could see some upside for the stock as it makes its recovery.

A robust pipeline

Sarepta generates its main revenue via the commercialization of its therapies for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. It is a rare genetic disorder in which boys experience muscular weakness at an early age. However, its main revenue driver is Elevidys, which was the first FDA-approved gene therapy for DMD. As of recent times, Elevidys has become Sarepta’s flagship product and now makes up the majority of its sales growth. The therapy itself has quite a high price point, at around $3.2 million per treatment. And whilst this seems extraordinarily high, this is often the case for one-time genetic disorder treatments. Recent label expansions have allowed the treatment of both ambulatory and non-ambulatory patients.

Aside from Elevidys, Sarepta also earns a substantial sum of its revenue from earlier RNA-based “exon skipping” drugs. These include Exondys 51, Vyondys 53, and Amondys 45. They still remain essential treatments for certain subsets, depending on their genetic mutation, of DMD patients. As most of you know, beyond their product sales, Sarepta has an ongoing collaboration with Roche. It gives them some additional revenue in the form of royalties and milestone payments for ex-U.S. Elevidys rights.

Now, Sarepta’s future growth, of course, depends on its clinical pipeline. They actually have over 40 investigational programs, but I’m going to choose to focus on their clinical-stage drugs, which are showing the most promise. They include several advanced-stage candidates nearing potential approval. By far the most advanced would be SRP-9003, which is a gene therapy for Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy Type 2E. They have already completed enrollment in its Phase 3 EMERGENE study. We should expect the data sometime in 2025.

For me, their biggest near-term catalyst would have to be SRP-9003, purely because of the fact that it would expand Sarepta’s presence way beyond Duchenne into another group of muscular dystrophies. Elsewhere, the company is moving forward with its next-generation siRNA therapies, SRP-1001, tipped ot be for Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy. SRP-1003, for Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1, is also being progressed. Both of these candidates are in Phase 1/2 trials via their collaboration with Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ARWR). These programs will be key in the near term as they are essentially Sarepta’s diversification into gene-silencing platforms. As they grow, they will require a gene-splicing platform to complement their gene therapy business. I put the most important therapies to focus on in the table below to get a sense of their clinical stage and what to look out for next.

2Q25 Review

For 2Q25, the company saw roughly $611.1 million in total revenue, up substantially from $362.9 million at the same time last year. It's a 68% year-over-year growth jump. On a non-GAAP adjusted basis, earnings per share came in at about $2.02, which was well above consensus expectations of $1.11. It's also sharply higher than the $0.07 EPS figure we saw a year ago. The company also reported some good operating profitability on both GAAP and a non-GAAP basis. In terms of cash flow, the quarter was also positive, with the company’s cash, cash equivalents, and investments increasing by roughly $202.8 million compared to the prior quarter. If we turn to costs, R&D plus SG&A (excluding non-cash items) rose a bit, but we can put that largely down to expanded clinical and collaboration activity.

As of last quarter, Sarepta held approximately $850 million in cash. As per their 10-Q, they list “total cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash” at about $526 million, but when you include the short- and long-term investments, the broader liquidity figure jumps to that $850 million mark. The big news out of last earnings was the restructuring plan, which they hope will reduce costs by about $400 million annually starting in 2025. They intend to refocus their pipeline and cut down some manufacturing commitments.

SRP-9003

As many investors already know and follow quite closely, SRP-9003 is the company's next promising near-term growth driver following Elevidys. It’s actually built on the same AAVrh74 vector platform, which is already used successfully in its Duchenne muscular dystrophy program. Now, what SRP-9003 does is that it delivers a functional copy of the β-sarcoglycan gene, which is the gene mutated in Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy, also called LGMDR4. The candidate has completed enrollment in the Phase 3 EMERGENE trial. This trial is meant to confirm the efficacy and any safety signals observed in earlier studies.

Now, we should expect the top-line results sometime in late 2025 or early 2026. And if results show promise, we can expect the company to submit a Biologics License Application to the FDA soon after. The trial’s main endpoints focused on β-sarcoglycan protein expression in muscle tissue, while other endpoints measured functional improvement over time. This included any motor strength and endurance. So investors will keep an eye out for that protein expression level and any improvement in motor strength. However, what we do have to give us an indication is early-phase data from the SRP-9003 program. It showed robust and sustained expression of β-sarcoglycan and thus improved functional outcomes. It also displayed a quite acceptable safety profile, which gave investors reason for optimism heading into the main readouts.

I expect the data press release to substantially move the stock in either direction depending on the outcome, so here's what to really focus on. For investors, the key things will be the magnitude and durability of the protein expression, the functional gains versus the baseline, and hopefully the absence of any major safety concerns. On the safety side of things, we don’t want to see things like liver toxicity or immune reactions. These are the kind of side effects that can really halt a pipeline candidate. We saw issues like this that have recently affected the company’s DMD gene therapy, Elevidys. The key to all this data is also keeping regulators happy. They’ll also scrutinize manufacturing consistency, given that AAV-based therapies must demonstrate lot-to-lot reliability, so the consistency of drug production across different batches. As for a timeline, I’m anticipating that positive Phase 3 data could pretty quickly translate into regulatory approval. This would set up SRP-9003 nicely as a second blockbuster gene therapy for Sarepta.

As for what's on offer, analysts are estimating the TAM for LGMD2E to fall somewhere just below 5,000 patients globally, since the incidence rate is about 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 350,000. After all, it’s only a small but highly lucrative rare-disease niche. In terms of price points, it's still very early days, but as I mentioned earlier with genetherapies, we usually see very high one-off prices. We should see a premium somewhat similar to Elevidys. So, a likely premium one-time price is in the $2 to $3 million range. Importantly, Sarepta is already commercially equipped to launch SRP-9003 efficiently, as it would follow a very similar marketing strategy to Elevidys.

Valuation

Sarepta is trading right now in the low $23 range. It holds a market cap of about $2.27 billion and an enterprise value of $2.84 billion. In terms of valuation, on a forward basis, the EV to sales ratio sits at 1.39x and the price to sales at 1.12x. Both are well below the sector medians of 3.80x and 4.28x, respectively. So Sarepta is being priced at quite a discount to peers, despite the company having a very advanced gene therapy pipeline in neuromuscular diseases. The forward price-to-book ratio is at 2.02x and also sits below the sector median of 2.99x, which tells us the stock is trading below the replacement value of its assets. Forward-looking price-to-earnings estimates for 2026 through 2027 are at 10.35x and 13.56x. These suggest a fairly modest multiple relative to future growth potential. I say modest here as we should consider the near-term catalysts from Elevidys and SRP-9003. EV to EBITDA is, of course, extremely high at 70.66x on a TTM basis. This is very typical for clinical-stage companies with hefty investments into R&D. However, for me, this is offset by the fact that Sarepta has a pretty strong cash position of $800 million.

Risks

The biggest here would be clinical and regulatory uncertainty around SRP-9003. I touched on safety concerns before, and we all saw it back in June. The company reported a second patient death as a result of acute liver failure. When nasty side effects like these are reported, it often hurts small pharma stocks a lot more than usual. Each time positive news is released on a drug stage or management hints towards success in earning the new market it begins to price in a positive data outcome from the upcoming trials. However, when we see harsh safety concerns, all those positives get priced out in one go, so we often see sharp sell-offs. Think of this also from an FDA point of view, they view the overall package of a candidate starting from the beginning and take all data points of studies leading into a decision. So with multiple patient deaths, they would, of course, be more inclined to extend or request more data later down the line.

Conclusion

So next to watch out for would be the SRP-9003 Phase 3 data that we’re all expecting soon. Management originally tipped it to come sometime in mid-2025. In terms of data, focus on the β-sarcoglycan expression, any functional gains, and those safety signals, such as liver toxicity. We may also get updates on Sarepta’s siRNA pipeline (SRP-1001 and SRP-1003) if there is a full press release when the data comes. Just before earnings, we have the World Muscle Society Congress in October to look forward to; we should expect an update on SGCB expression and safety then, which I'll be watching. 3Q25 earnings are also coming up soon and should fall in early November.
